text: The patient is positioned supine following general anesthesia and intubation. The first trocar is inserted in the left upper quadrant using Hassan's technique, and the peritoneal cavity is insufflated. A second 5mm trocar is placed under direct laparoscopic vision. The laparoscopic view reveals an enlarged uterus occupying the pelvis and lower abdomen. Elongated loose bands attached to the distal edge of the omentum above the uterus are identified and divided. Additional 5mm trocars are inserted to enhance maneuverability. The exploration attempts to locate Treitz's ligament, but it is obscured by the enlarged uterus. The bowel loops appear fragile and edematous due to preobstructive dilation, and a bowel grasper is used to maintain intestinal integrity and prevent injury.

The surgeon identifies the trite's ligament and continues exploration towards the ileocecal valve. The exploration is paused due to the incarceration of a small intestine segment, prompting a change in viewing angle to examine distal parts. Upon detecting the pathology, the intestines are re-evaluated from the terminal ileum to the lesion, with no additional pathology observed.

Congenital bands located approximately ten centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve are visible, originating from the right lateral abdominal wall and extending towards the cecum. These bands are divided using ligature, reducing the internal herniation and freeing the passage. The blood circulation to the jejunal segment appears sufficient, with no additional intra-abdominal pathology observed.
